EI-01: VR - liked the most; immersed you in a task and made you feel like you were a part of it and not just looking at the setting. Did not enjoy AR, felt like it was disorienting and it was difficult to focus on the task with the outside world. Did not enjoy the technology of MR and does not feel like it is caught up. Did not enjoy the functionality.
EI-02: Yes, only for VR. States that as an individual who is a recovering addict, when you feel like you are a part of something like he did when he was experiencing VR, then it makes recovery easier and makes you feel like you are not the problem. Also felt the most security in VR and didn't feel judged. Felt like someone was actually trying to help him.
EI-03: Feels like it's up to the patient to identify if they want help and the app would primarily be beneficial to those who are wanting help. It's good to have the app as a cravings management tool because it is also convenient, as a lot of people are on their phones these days.
EI-04: CBT - felt like the videos were undermining and made it seem like the producers think that "addicts are stupid." The way the woman in the videos spoke did not seem genuine. HRV - participant notes that he personally never really got anything out of breathing exercises ever, so he's not the best to talk about his experience with this particular intervention
EI-05: yes - feels like it would be beneficial to have the app available to individuals after a 30-day rehab into OUD treatment plans? program (for example) and have the app available to continue treatment and progressing towards sobriety outside of the facility setting.
EI-06: yes - would use on a daily basis. Believes that one would have to use it daily if they are wanting to remain clean.

EI-01: felt that MR and VR were easy to use and navigate, realistic, and comfortable. enjoyed AR the most and noted that it was the most comfortable and easiest on the eyes and easiest to navigate and use.
EI-02: yes - especially the breathing technique. feels that the interventions with these technologies should mimic the same format as the study's and believes that the breathing exercises in all technologies would be the most beneficial.
EI-03: believes the app was easy to use and is a great cravings management tool. asked the right questions and generated appropriate interventions. does not believe questionnaire was too long or too short.
EI-04: CBT - enjoyed them and found them soothing and relatable. does not believe that they were too long or too short. enjoyed HRV and worked well for patient personally, did not find them too long or too short. felt that both HRV and CBT interventions were appropriate for intensity level reported.
EI-05: yes - believes that it would be good for patients to have this treatment intervention through their phones that they could access, especially in situations of crisis and feels that it would also be great if the app could be linked to other devices (computer, tablet, watch, etc)

EI-01: augmented reality was the least liked and virtual reality was the most liked. 3about mixed reality. liked virtual reality because pt could feel that they could focus more on the interventions and themselves. felt like all headsets were easy to navigate, but pretty touch sensitive. rates AR 5/10, MR 7/10, and VR 9/10.
EI-02: somewhat, yes. the tech helped a little, but it was nice to have the interventions to be somewhere and have some sense of escape from reality and that is what individuals with OUD may need incorporated into treatment.
EI-03: helps a little, gives someone something to focus on other than using substances. sometimes it's helpful to be by yourself and not talk to anyone and focus on yourself and what interventions you may need.
EI-04: CBT - did not enjoy. did not feel like the videos felt real or genuine. HRV: really did enjoy. and allowed pt to escape. thinks that if people had a minute to just focus on themselves entirely and breathe, then they may be able to stop thinking about using or actually using drugs. felt like CBT was not appropriate for triggers nor helpful, but felt like HRV was significantly helpful for her.
EI-05: yes. it helps by giving individuals something to do and put their thoughts down and delve deeper into them. it is helpful to focus without being around people. pt states that they are not a people person.
EI-06: yes. would utilize one to two times per day.

EI-01: virtual reality was the favorite since it was the most immersive experience, fun and easy to use and understand. enjoyed mixed reality, but not as much due to less immersive and did not compare and also rating these based on tech comparisons. did not enjoy augmented reality at all, as it was not as immersive and i was aware of my real surroundings.
EI-02: potentially if the patient is ready to quit and wants to receive help, especially with the breathing exercises. utilizing these technologies may be beneficial depending on the severity of the patient.
EI-03: there would have to be mild cravings for there to be a positive impact on an individual. i do not believe that these interventions would be very beneficial if someone was having more severe cravings.
EI-04: did not enjoy cbt at all. we are used to hearing everything this woman has said to us from others and in classes and it's not helpful. she makes it sound so easy to stop and kind of makes you feel worse as a person if you do not stop. HRV was very calming depending on the type of immersion session you are exhibiting. CBT not appropriate for intensity level and triggers reported, HRV more appropriate.
EI-05: yes once someone is considered stable. patient defines stabilities as no more physical cravings or symptoms of withdrawal.
EI-06: potentially, does not really know due to really only enjoying interventions through immersive experiences and not just on the phone.

EI-01: VR was the favorite, had a lot of fun with virtual reality. felt like i could concentrate more because i couldn't see the surroundings, 10/10. AR was more cumbersome and not as comfortable nor immersive and was more distracting, 5/10. MR was a slight step up from AR and i felt 36.5/10
EI-02: absolutely, 100%. based on own experiences, i feel like meditation is incredibly important to creating new neuropathways and changing behaviors in general. could see all of technologies being effective as long as you're in the right headspace.
EI-03: application was easy to use, pretty straight forward and effective. felt like the answers correlated well with the videos and exercises.
EI-04: yes, i found the videos to be helpful. the little reminders can be helpful sometimes, specifically with one of the loneliness videos. the breathing exercises were helpful and allowed me to take a minute to get grounded.
EI-05:
i don't see why not, yes. i think that it has the potential to help individuals with OUD and any intervention or tool that could benefit someone should be utilized. it would be good in teaching people how to breathe, meditate, and cope since
they may not know
EI-06: yes. would utilize once a day to manage cravings.

EI-01: believes that both augmented and mixed realities were cool and had structure, but enjoyed the virtual reality the most. believes that it would have the most impact on recovery treatment and was the most immersive.
EI-02: believes that virtual reality would have the most positive impact. believes that it would allow participants to have a space to relax and listening to the videos while focusing.
EI-03: believes that the app covers the bases of the aspects that are important for recovery, but believes that in order for it to be effective, the patient must be fully aware of their needs.
EI-04: CBT - believes that it was helpful. notes that it was hard to initially settle in and listen to what the individual in the video was saying, but believes that everything that she said was important and helpful. HRV - very helpful. believes that the the exercise allowed him to get focused and calm and allowed him to settle into the type of reality that he was experiencing. believes that it should be the first intervention that is done, prior to watching CBT videos.
EI-05: yes, but only if a patient is motivated and willing to share their accurate emotions honestly
EI-06: yes, would definitely give it a try, especially after experiencing it through the study. could envision self utilizing the app at least once a week.

EI-01: The CBT " I enjoyed it " . She enjoyed VR but did not like the breathing aspect. The VR headset was too heavy. She enjoyed both augmented and mixed more than VR based on the weight of the equipment.
EI-02: Yes, this may be an alternative for people that do not want the human interaction. Specifically, those still actively using opioids.
EI-03: She believes it was effective because of how the survey match her to an intervention that worked for her.
EI-04: She felt they were effective because of the person-centered approach.
EI-05: Yes, it should be available to people in all type of OUD tx.

EI-01: enjoyed VR the most because it actually felt like the participant was going to a different place. disliked both AR and VR. felt like it interfered with vision and caused difficulty focusing on the task because of being so aware of the surroundings.
EI-02: yes - only VR. could envision VR being used to calm an individual down during crisis, especially with the breathing exercise. if an individual was truly focusing, the CBT videos could be helpful as well.
EI-03: feels like the app could be useful because it took the participant's mind off of the cravings, believes the questions asked were appropriate but feels like more questions could have been asked and could have delved deeper.
EI-04: CBT - feels like it would be helpful to an individual that is new to recovery. feels like the intervention was not super beneficial for them because they have been in recovery for so long and have heard the same things in the videos before. HRV - felt like the intervention was too long. could have been helpful to have a break in between and feels like it may be helpful to incorporate different breathing exercises into the activity, such as the 4x4 breathing exercise.
EI-05:
yes - feels like it would be beneficial to take it home and utilize it daily or whenever they are feeling increased tension or anxiety. feels like this should be something that is utilized as needing depending on the individual patient and
their stability
EI-06: yes - could envision self utilizing the app a couple of times per week

EI-01: VR was the best one. Was able to get a real feel to it because it blocked everything out. Did not enjoy AR or MR much because could still see external environment.
EI-02: Yes, with the VR for sure. Could see it helping someone since it blocks everything around you and gets your full attention.
EI-03: It's good at getting a feel of what's going on. It could be better as far as giving more interventions and exercises to go with it.
EI-04: CBT: Feels like the woman in the videos hit on almost every point, but could have gone a little more in depth with the subjects that she was discussing. HRV - did help participant. Believes that these interventions were appropriate for the intensity level and triggers reported.
EI-05:
Yes - could see the iPAL helping, specifically with assisting with understanding and navigating one's
feelings.
EI-06: Yes - could envision self using the application pretty frequently (4-5x per week)

EI-01: believes that all headsets were easy to navigate. MR - felt like there was more of a real image than artificial image and believes that it would be the easiest to navigate. participant states that they actually felt like they were on the beach during the HRV intervention. VR - enjoyed the visuals and enjoyed being able to get a fuller picture of everything. believes that the imaging was too animated and that the headset itself was too bulky. AR - did not enjoy at all, but believes that aspect was nice. states that it was a bit more difficult to navigate initially and visuals were more difficult to see.
EI-02: does not believe that they would be beneficial during crisis mode in a real life scenario. states that it would work if these headsets were available, but does not think that this is logical or realistic due to how expensive these headsets are. notes that it could help, but there has to be practicality when developing a treatment plan and making these interventions more accessible.
EI-03: asked the right questions and was not too long or too short. hit the main points that are important
EI-04: CBT - perfect length, not too long or too short; CBT - perfect length, not too long or too short; thinking about aspects of life in different ways. notes that the videos personally did not help the participant with the intensity of their cravings, but helped identify triggers for cravings. HRV - very good intervention; helped calm down and get in the right headspace and believes it was a perfect length. both interventions reported to be appropriate for intensity level and triggers reported.
EI-05: yes - could definitely assist in identifying triggers and allowing space for self. reports that identifying triggers is one of the hardest things to do for someone in addiction and some people don't know/can't identify why they like getting high, so this could be very beneficial for someone in active addiction.
EI-06: yes - could envision self utilizing app 3-4x/week

EI-01: didn't care for AR much, felt like paid attention to external environment too much to actually pay attention to intervention. felt like MR was okay and the easiest to use of the three. VR was the favorite because it was the most immersive and gave illusion that participant was present.
EI-02: yes - feels like it would be significantly better to use these interventions for individuals as opposed to the standard care with a person, varies on person/patient
EI-03: feels like it was a good intervention with good questions asked and not too many or too few questions. allows the individual using the app to acknowledge what and how different factors are affecting them.
EI-04: CBT - only enjoyed in VR intervention. felt like the videos were too short, but felt like they were appropriate for the intensity level and triggers reported. HRV - enjoyed a lot and felt calm. feels like the intervention would be nice if it were a bit longer and believes the interventions were appropriate for the intensity level and triggers reported.
EI-05: yes - could be either used in therapy or at home as needed for triggers or even on a regular basis, depending on the patient and their stability.
EI-06: yes - could envision self utilizing app twice per day

EI-01: participant notes that he enjoyed his experiences with all of the headsets, but enjoyed VR the most. enjoyed undergoing the interventions in all three of them.
EI-02: yes, especially for those who prefer listening to someone speaking as opposed to going to an actual counselor and speaking with them. there is also the convenience of potentially using these technologies at home with the iPAL app, but participant is unsure of how this could potentially be executed.
EI-03: believes that the app was great and easy to use and navigate and believes that it asked appropriate questions.
EI-04: CBT - believes that the videos were "alright." felt like they were appropriate for the intensity level and triggers reported. does not believe that they were too long or too short. believes the breathing exercises were appropriate for the triggers reported, but believes that they were too long, but may be beneficial and a good length for other patients.
EI-05: yes, but believes it would be most beneficial to use with the headsets as opposed to independently
EI-06: yes - could envision self utilizing once per week, yes - could envision self utilizing once per week,

EI-01:
notes that all of the interventions were nice in all of their own ways and states that he enjoyed VR the most due to how detailed and clear the graphics were in comparison to the graphics visualized in MR
and AR.
EI-02: yes - could see these as a potential replacement to AA/NA meetings or therapy for those who don't want seek treatment due to lack of accessibility to care or fear of judgement.
EI-03: did not personally enjoy the app much, but states that could see it being beneficial for others. asked the appropriate amount of questions. could not really elaborate on why they did not personally enjoy it much.
EI-04: CBT - feels like the intervention was okay. was too short and basic and feels like the way that the individual in the video was talking was not personalized. HRV - felt like the intervention was okay and helpful for individuals who need to focus on easing stress if emotions and anxiety are high. felt like both interventions were appropriate for the intensity level and triggers reported.
EI-05: yes - believes that the app could be beneficial only if i the patient is willing to put the work in.

EI-01: enjoyed VR the most due to it being the most immersive. can definitely tell it's virtual and not real, but feels like you could potentially put yourself there if you were comfortable enough. did not enjoy MR much and felt that it was difficult to focus on the tasks. disliked AR the most and felt like it was incredibly difficult to focus on the task while being able to see the background.
EI-02: yes - only VR. AR could be very aggravating for people and feels like VR could be beneficial for individuals that could be having a very difficult day with cravings at a 10. helpful to take a moment through VR and get their minds off of what is going on.
EI-03: believes that it is a good cravings management tool. the questions make you aware of what's going on in your life on a daily basis and how you're feeling and what's causing those feelings. could be helpful for feelings management as well and could potentially help with linking a cause behind the feelings.
EI-04: CBT - enjoyed the videos a lot. whenever someone is made aware of those feelings and cravings they are experiencing and understanding how to manage them, this could be helpful in recovery. participant feels that sometimes they have difficulty with remembering to check in with themselves and what's going on. HRV - felt like the exercise was too long. feels like it may have been a good length to keep someone grounded if coordinator wasn't in the room during the intervention.
EI-05: yes - there are multiple pathways for a reason and participant believes that if the application were presented as an option, it could be very beneficial to others in recovery. should be utilized when people are coming into recovery and getting out of treatment.
EI-06: yes - would envision utilizing twice per week

EI-01: believes that all of the realities were all easy to navigate and very relaxing to use. enjoyed the virtual reality the most due to how realistic it seems and how time slipped away during usage.
EI-02: yes - believes that if someone is experiencing cravings, then these technologies could assist with putting them in a different state of mind and help as a preventative method of treatment.
EI-03: enjoyed using the app, believes that it is very straightforward and easy to use. notes that a good amount of questions were asked and good questions were asked as well. no suggestions reported.
EI-04: believes that CBT videos were very helpful. felt like they hit on the right points and did not believe that they were either too long or too short. also believes that HRV was very helpful as well and also a good length of time. believes that both interventions were appropriate for the intensity level and triggers reported.
EI-05: yes, believes that just like the virtual, augmented, and mixed reality interventions, the app could help provide interventions through the phone that could assist with putting someone in a different state of mind when struggling with feelings of cravings.
EI-06: yes, reports that would use the app once a week

EI-01: AR - believes that it was easy to navigate and the most simple. enjoyed using this headset a lot. MR - enjoyed the most and felt like it was also very easy to navigate. VR - disliked the most. participant reports that this was very overstimulating and participant reports having a panic attack during the intervention. could not tolerate for greater than 2 minutes.
EI-02: yes - this could be helpful for individuals who don't socialize much with others and allow for options for other outlets to help with managing cravings.
EI-03: believes that the app was helpful and covered all important aspects. states that good interventions came were developed based on the answers to her questions.
EI-04: CBT - good videos, but felt as if they were a bit monotone and a little too long. believes that they were appropriate for intensity levels reported. HRV - believes that these were too long, but somewhat helpful. believes that both interventions were appropriate for the intensity level and triggers reported.
EI-05: yes - believes that the app was adequate and easy to navigate. believes that it was a good cravings management tool.
EI-06: doesn't envision self personally using due to having previously been in a significant amount of outpatient treatment. believes that these questions would get redundant and interventions would get boring after a while and participant also believes that they are currently in a good place in recovery to where they don't feel like these current interventions would be very beneficial.

EI-01: VR - very cumbersome; bulky and put a lot of pressure and strain on back of neck. feels that if there were some more support, it would be a more ideal headset with the best digital experience while being comfortable. AR - least immersive, but also the best on the body, felt like it was "okay." MR - felt like it was really confusing, but also really enjoyed the graphics and sounds.
EI-02:
yes - if someone were to go through a program, it would benefit them to get out of their comfort zone in a short amount of time. personally helped this participant because they do not like dealing with and being around people and would rather deal with
interventions virtually but notes that the downside would be not being held to the same amount of accountability. may be helpful to have someone on the outside monitoring while also keeping the interventions between patient and technology
private
EI-03: easy to use, but thinks that someone could lie pretty easily and manipulate results unless questions are asked in different ways. would also be good to add questions that could gauge social determinants of health to get the full picture of what is going on with the individual (housing, employment) and also asking questions about their wellbeing (current depression/anxiety)
EI-04: CBT - mixture of like and dislike; felt like it was not personal, but it was also nice to not have someone "dissect their life." HRV - disliked, felt like it was too long and participant was unable to participate throughout the full intervention due to being a long-term smoker. feels like it would be helpful to have different variations of breathing exercises that are more helpful for individuals with lower lung capacity.
EI-05: yes - feels like it should be available for individuals in an outpatient setting during initial visits with providers so that their providers can holistically get to know their patients on a deeper level without the patient feeling uncomfortable.
EI-06: notes that participant personally wouldn't do it if it was not recommended by their provider, but would if their provider recommended it. would use as much as recommended by provider.

EI-01: AR - good experience, still saw a lot of surroundings, had mixed feelings about this specific intervention, but believes that it was good overall. enjoyed the least out of the other interventions. VR - enjoyed the most. felt fully immersed in experience and felt like the counselor from the CBT interventions was right in front of him. notes that pt got lost in this experience. MR: enjoyed as well and notes that his senses were very heightened during this experience. still aware of surroundings, but felt as if there were somehow two different realities.
EI-02: yes, especially VR. believes these technologies can get someone out of reality and be fully immersed in other tasks. feels that cravings would definitely be eased with these interventions, especially with the one on one CBT counselor. notes that these technologies would be creating a beneficial distraction if they were utilized in treatment.
EI-03: believes that it is a great tool if someone were to answer truthfully. notes that it could be a little more detailed and ask more questions. pt notes that they personally never had high cravings when completing the questionnaire and notes that if they maybe had higher cravings, it would be helpful to ask more in depth questions about the nature of the cravings.
EI-04: CBT - felt that intervention was calming and perfect for the level of cravings pt reported. felt like the individual in the video was sympathizing with pt even though she wasn't there. noted that pt felt like the videos were a little too short. HRV - loved the breathing exercises and found them very beneficial. helped pt to recenter themselves and found this to be a great tool. not too long or too short.
EI-05: yes - creates a good distraction and feels that this could be used outside of the doctor office setting. anytime cravings are bad for an individual and they are unable to get to a doctor's or therapist's office due to barriers, it would be great to utilize.
EI-06: yes - could envision self utilizing 4x/week at least and if not, everyday. feels that every individual always needs to center and calm self.

EI-01: enjoyed MR the most. notes that it fit right and was much easier to navigate than the other two headsets. felt like VR was okay, but too heavy and was more difficult to navigate. enjoyed AR the least and states that pt did not feel like it was immersive at all nor impactful and states that it was more aggravating to navigate.
EI-02: yes, especially MR. other interventions are more person-specific, but believes these technologies have a plan in OUD tx and management and notes that they were very peaceful and relaxing for pt.
EI-03: believes the app was simple and asked appropriate questions. did not feel as if the survey was too long or too short and believes that it is an appropriate cravings management tool.
EI-04: HRV - intervention was relaxing and peaceful and they were smooth and easy to do. does not believe the intervention was either too long or too short. CBT - participant did not experience these interventions and they were not recommended for pt, so n/a.
EI-05: yes - feels that the app would be similar to having  a sponsor or a helpful outlet. feels like it would be great to have this tool to use on personal time at home to manage cravings, especially when those cravings are high.
EI-06: yes - frequency would depend on how much the pt would feel the need to utilize
